event: Mehrgarh, one of the earliest farming and herding settlements in South Asia, is established on the Kacchi Plain in Balochistan.
The Indus Valley Civilization reaches its mature urban phase, with major cities such as Harappa and Mohenjo-daro featuring advanced drainage and grid planning.
The mature Indus Valley Civilization declines as its major cities are gradually abandoned.
Indo-Aryan-speaking peoples settle the Punjab region and the early Vedic culture takes root in the northwest.
Darius I of the Persian Achaemenid Empire annexes the Indus Valley as the satrapy of Hindush.
Alexander the Great defeats King Porus at the Battle of the Hydaspes (Jhelum) River, extending Greek influence into the Punjab.
Chandragupta Maurya secures the Indus region from the Seleucids, bringing it under the Mauryan Empire.
Buddhism flourishes in Gandhara under Mauryan patronage, with Taxila becoming a renowned center of learning.
Indo-Greek kingdoms rise in the northwest as Greco-Bactrian rulers expand into Gandhara and the Punjab.
Indo-Scythian (Saka) rulers conquer much of the Indus region from the Indo-Greeks.
The Kushan Empire under Kanishka the Great makes Gandhara a major hub of trade, art, and Mahayana Buddhism.
The Hephthalites (White Huns) invade and disrupt the northwest, weakening established kingdoms in the Indus region.
Umayyad general Muhammad bin Qasim conquers Sindh and southern Punjab, introducing Islam to the region.
Mahmud of Ghazni begins a series of campaigns into the Punjab, incorporating it into the Ghaznavid Empire.
Muhammad of Ghor overthrows the Ghaznavids at Lahore, paving the way for Muslim rule across northern South Asia.
The Delhi Sultanate is founded, bringing the Punjab and Sindh under successive Turkic and Afghan dynasties.
Timur invades and sacks Delhi, devastating the Punjab on his march through the region.
Babur defeats the Delhi Sultanate at the First Battle of Panipat, founding the Mughal Empire that rules the region.
Akbar's reign begins, ushering in a Mughal golden age with Lahore serving as a major imperial city.
The Badshahi Mosque is completed in Lahore under Aurangzeb, epitomizing Mughal architecture in the region.
Nader Shah of Persia invades and sacks the Mughal northwest, accelerating Mughal decline.
Ranjit Singh captures Lahore and establishes the Sikh Empire across the Punjab.
The British East India Company conquers Sindh after the Battle of Miani.
Britain annexes the Punjab following victory in the Second Anglo-Sikh War, completing colonial control of the region.
The Indian Rebellion against British rule erupts, after which the Crown assumes direct governance of British India.
The Indian National Congress is founded, beginning organized political mobilization across British India.
The All-India Muslim League is founded in Dhaka to advocate for the political interests of Muslims.
Poet Muhammad Iqbal delivers his Allahabad address envisioning a separate Muslim state in northwestern India.
The Muslim League adopts the Lahore Resolution, formally demanding independent states for Muslim-majority regions.
Pakistan gains independence from Britain on 14 August with Muhammad Ali Jinnah as first Governor-General, amid the partition of India and mass migration.
The First Kashmir War breaks out between Pakistan and India over the disputed princely state of Jammu and Kashmir.
Pakistan is admitted to the United Nations on 30 September.
Founder Muhammad Ali Jinnah dies, leaving the new nation to consolidate under his successors.
Pakistan adopts its first constitution and becomes an Islamic Republic.
General Ayub Khan seizes power in a military coup, beginning the country's first period of military rule.
The Second Indo-Pakistani War is fought over Kashmir, ending in a UN-brokered ceasefire.
East Pakistan secedes after a civil war and the Indo-Pakistani War, becoming the independent nation of Bangladesh.
A new constitution establishes a parliamentary system with Zulfikar Ali Bhutto as Prime Minister.
General Zia-ul-Haq overthrows Bhutto in a coup and launches a program of Islamization.
Former Prime Minister Zulfikar Ali Bhutto is executed after a controversial trial.
Benazir Bhutto becomes Prime Minister, the first woman to head a government in a Muslim-majority country, after Zia dies in a plane crash.
Pakistan conducts nuclear weapons tests in Balochistan, becoming a declared nuclear power.
General Pervez Musharraf seizes power in a bloodless coup, ousting Prime Minister Nawaz Sharif.
Former Prime Minister Benazir Bhutto is assassinated while campaigning in Rawalpindi.
Musharraf resigns and civilian democratic rule is restored following national elections.
Catastrophic monsoon flooding submerges roughly a fifth of the country, affecting some 20 million people.
Al-Qaeda leader Osama bin Laden is killed in a US raid in Abbottabad.
Pakistan completes its first democratic transition between elected civilian governments.
Militants attack an Army Public School in Peshawar, killing nearly 150 people, most of them children.
Devastating floods inundate a third of the country, displacing millions amid a deepening economic crisis.
